region_of_origin: Global (widely consumed across South Asia, Middle East, Mediterranean, and Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a semolina food product class; cooked form is not separately enumerated. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Wheat semolina, cooked — mapped to FoodEx2 grain-based cooked product category under cereals and cereal products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated cooked semolina entry; closest defensible code under cooked cereal products used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cooked semolina as a food product; SNOMED CT food coding is limited for generic grain preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of cooked semolina is approximately 180g (one medium bowl), providing about 178 kcal, 35g carbohydrate, and 6g protein.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked Semolina — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cooked semolina (cr_id F05080009). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical source with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cooked semolina GI is estimated at approximately 55–65 (midpoint 60) based on published GI values for cooked semolina and durum wheat semolina products in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Semolina pasta and cooked semolina entries in the Sydney GI database report GI values in the 55–65 range. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 35.3) / 100 = 21.2, rounded to 21.1 for 180g serving (19.6g carb per 100g × 1.8 = 35.3g carb).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3mg/100g) with no added salt, sugar, or saturated fat in plain cooked form
hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked semolina is suitable for hypertension management due to its very low sodium content (3mg/100g); however, patients should avoid adding salt, butter, or high-sodium sauces, and should pair it with potassium-rich foods such as legumes or vegetables to support DASH dietary goals, as potassium content is low at 36mg/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~60) with 19.6g carbohydrate and low dietary fibre (1.0g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked semolina has a moderate GI (~60) and is acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes with strict portion control (recommended ≤150g cooked per meal); dietary fibre is low at 1.0g/100g, so pairing with high-fibre legumes or non-starchy vegetables is strongly advised to reduce glycemic load, and sweet preparations such as semolina pudding with sugar or condensed milk must be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.0g/100g), low total fat (0.4g/100g), and zero cholesterol in plain cooked form
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain cooked semolina poses low cardiovascular risk due to negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol; however, it is a refined grain with low fibre (1.0g/100g), so it should be consumed in moderate portions and paired with fibre-rich vegetables or legumes to support heart health, and preparations with ghee, butter, or cream should be avoided.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined grain with no meaningful anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, or fermented components)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cooked semolina is not recommended as a primary grain choice for rheumatoid arthritis due to its refined nature and absence of anti-inflammatory factors; patients should substitute with whole-grain alternatives such as whole wheat semolina or quinoa, and if consuming, pair with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as turmeric, ginger, and colourful vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked semolina is a soft, pale yellow porridge-like food made by boiling durum wheat semolina (coarse-ground wheat middlings) in water until it absorbs the liquid and becomes a smooth, creamy, mildly flavoured grain dish. It has a gentle, slightly nutty wheat taste and a soft, smooth texture that makes it easy to eat and digest. Semolina is widely consumed across South Asia (as suji/sooji/rava), the Middle East, the Mediterranean, and Southeast Asia, and appears in both savoury and sweet preparations. The plain cooked form contains approximately 99 kcal per 100g, with 19.6g carbohydrate, 3.4g protein, and only 0.4g fat, making it a low-fat, moderate-energy grain. Sodium is very low at 3mg/100g, making it suitable for low-sodium diets. Dietary fibre is modest at 1.0g/100g. The glycemic index is moderate (~60), meaning blood glucose rises at a moderate rate; portion control is important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is naturally free of cholesterol and sugar in its plain cooked form. Cooked semolina is suitable for soft-food diets, post-illness recovery, and as a gentle breakfast or side dish. It contains gluten and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance.
common_user_queries: Is cooked semolina good for diabetes?
How many calories in cooked semolina?
Can I eat semolina with high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of semolina?
Is cooked semolina good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05080009) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008) for cooked semolina and durum wheat semolina products, which consistently report GI in the 55–65 range.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; plain cooked semolina contains negligible saturated fat. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; plain cooked semolina contains no added or intrinsic sugar. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived grain product with no animal-derived ingredients. The following fields are set to -1 because the HPB Foodo database entry F05080009 does not individually report these values, and no supplementary approved source was used as primary evidence to preserve HPB source integrity: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported in HPB F05080009; polyunsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported in HPB F05080009; trans_fat_g — trans fat not reported in HPB F05080009; vitamin_c_mg — not reported in HPB F05080009; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported in HPB F05080009; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — not reported in HPB F05080009; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — not reported in HPB F05080009; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — not reported in HPB F05080009; vitamin_b6_mg — not reported in HPB F05080009; vitamin_b12_mcg — not reported in HPB F05080009; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported in HPB F05080009; vitamin_d_mcg — not reported in HPB F05080009; vitamin_e_mg — not reported in HPB F05080009; phosphorus_mg — not reported in HPB F05080009; magnesium_mg — not reported in HPB F05080009; zinc_mg — not reported in HPB F05080009; selenium_mcg — not reported in HPB F05080009; water_g — moisture content not reported in HPB F05080009. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 20066, Semolina, enriched, cooked) provides partial micronutrient data but was not used as primary source to preserve HPB source integrity; supplementary USDA data may be used in future enrichment. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 published values for cooked semolina and durum wheat semolina products (range 55–65). Glycemic load per serving calculated for 180g serving: (60 × 35.28) / 100 = 21.2 GL units. This food contains gluten (wheat) and is not suitable for coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity. The food is classified as a generic record (not a Singapore-specific dish), so Singaporean multilingual naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply; however, Hindi/romanized aliases (suji, sooji, rava) are included given widespread South Asian usage context.
